does: This paper systematically evaluates multilingual jailbreak vulnerabilities in major LLMs by translating harmful prompts into low-resource African languages and testing them through both automated methods and human red-teaming. The authors examine single-turn and multi-turn conversation strategies across ChatGPT, Claude, DeepSeek, Gemini, and Grok, discovering that while direct translation attacks fail, multi-turn conversations in Afrikaans, Kiswahili, isiXhosa, and isiZulu achieve jailbreak success rates ranging from 42% to 84% depending on the language and model. They demonstrate that human red-teaming consistently outperforms automated testing, increasing average jailbreak rates from 60% to 76% across all languages, and identify poor translation quality as a limiting factor in attack effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on established research showing that LLM safety mechanisms rely heavily on English-language training data and can be circumvented through prompt injection and jailbreaking techniques. It extends prior findings that linguistic gaps create vulnerabilities by focusing specifically on underrepresented African languages where safety training is minimal. The paper responds to growing awareness that commercial LLMs encode language-specific safety biases and builds on multilingual adversarial attack literature by combining translation-based attacks with multi-turn conversation strategies. It leverages existing jailbreak prompt datasets and adapts established red-teaming methodologies to the multilingual setting, drawing on human evaluation practices used in prior safety research.
enables: This research highlights a critical gap in the safety deployment of global LLMs and motivates urgent work on equitable multilingual safety alignment. It points toward better evaluation frameworks that stress-test models across linguistically diverse attack surfaces and suggests that robust safeguards require training on non-English harmful content. The findings enable development of more comprehensive safety benchmarks covering low-resource languages and support arguments for improved translation quality in safety training data. Future work could explore whether similar vulnerabilities exist in other underrepresented language families, how multilingual fine-tuning affects jailbreak resistance, and whether language-agnostic safety mechanisms could provide more robust defenses across the world's linguistic diversity.
